quiz_questions.question_text: What is the difference between distance and displacement?

options.text: Distance is total path length (scalar), displacement is straight-line change in position (vector)
Displacement is always larger than distance
They are the same thing measured differently
Distance has direction, displacement does not

quiz_questions.explanation: Distance measures the total length of the path traveled (scalar, always positive). Displacement measures the straight-line distance from start to finish with direction (vector, can be positive, negative, or zero).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is average speed?

options.text: Total distance traveled divided by total time taken
Final speed minus initial speed
The speed at a single instant
Total displacement divided by total time

quiz_questions.explanation: Average speed = total distance / total time. It is a scalar quantity and is always positive.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is average velocity?

options.text: The velocity at one instant
Total distance divided by total time
The sum of all velocities divided by the count
Displacement divided by total time taken

quiz_questions.explanation: Average velocity = Δx / Δt (displacement / time). It is a vector quantity and can be positive, negative, or zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car travels 100 km north then 100 km south. What is its distance and displacement?

options.text: Both are 200 km
Distance = 200 km, displacement = 0 km
Both are 100 km
Distance = 0 km, displacement = 200 km

quiz_questions.explanation: Distance is the total path: 100 + 100 = 200 km. Displacement is the net change in position: 100 km north − 100 km south = 0 km (back to start).

quiz_questions.question_text: What is instantaneous speed?

options.text: The maximum speed reached
The speed of an object at a specific moment in time
The speed at the start of a journey
The average of all speeds during a trip

quiz_questions.explanation: Instantaneous speed is the rate of distance change at a particular instant. It corresponds to the magnitude of the slope of a position-time graph at that point.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can a moving object have zero velocity?

options.text: No — a moving object always has velocity
Not instantaneously while moving, but an object can have zero average velocity after a round trip
Yes — if it moves in a circle forever
Only at the speed of light

quiz_questions.explanation: An object completing a round trip has zero average velocity (zero displacement / time). However, at any instant during motion, instantaneous velocity is nonzero. At a turnaround point, instantaneous velocity momentarily equals zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the SI unit of speed?

options.text: Meters per second (m/s)
Kilometers per hour (km/h)
Miles per hour (mph)
Feet per second (ft/s)

quiz_questions.explanation: The SI unit of speed (and velocity) is meters per second (m/s). Other units like km/h can be converted: divide by 3.6 to convert km/h to m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: A jogger runs 5 km in 25 minutes. What is their average speed in km/h?

options.text: 12 km/h
5 km/h
0.2 km/h
25 km/h

quiz_questions.explanation: Average speed = distance / time = 5 km / (25/60 h) = 5 / 0.417 = 12 km/h.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the slope of a position-time graph represent?

options.text: Acceleration
Distance
Velocity
Force

quiz_questions.explanation: The slope of a position-time graph gives velocity. A steeper slope means higher velocity; a horizontal line means zero velocity (stationary). A negative slope indicates motion in the negative direction.

quiz_questions.question_text: An object moves 30 m east in 5 s. What is its velocity?

options.text: 150 m/s east
25 m/s east
6 m/s east
6 m/s (direction unknown)

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity = displacement / time = 30 m east / 5 s = 6 m/s east. Since velocity is a vector, the direction must be included.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes distance?

options.text: The straight-line separation between two points
Displacement measured without direction
The total length of the path traveled by an object, regardless of direction — a scalar quantity
The area under a velocity-time graph

quiz_questions.explanation: Distance is scalar (no direction) and equals the sum of all path segments. It is always positive or zero and can never decrease during motion.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes displacement?

options.text: The total length of the path an object travels during its motion, measured along every twist and turn — a scalar quantity
The change in position of an object measured as a straight line from initial to final position, with direction — a vector quantity
The rate at which an object changes its location over a given time interval, expressed in meters per second — a kinematic rate
The product of an object's average speed and the elapsed travel time, yielding the total ground covered — measured in meters

quiz_questions.explanation: Displacement = Δx = x_final − x_initial. It has magnitude (shortest distance between start and end) and direction. It can be positive, negative, or zero.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes speed?

options.text: The rate at which an object covers distance — a scalar quantity equal to distance divided by time
The magnitude of displacement per unit time — a vector quantity that includes the direction of travel along the path
The product of distance and the time elapsed during motion, measured in meter-seconds as a compound kinematic unit
The instantaneous rate of change of displacement, accounting for both the magnitude and the direction of the motion

quiz_questions.explanation: Speed = distance/time. It is always positive or zero, has no direction, and tells how fast (but not in what direction) an object is moving.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes velocity?

options.text: Acceleration multiplied by time
Speed with an arbitrary direction assigned
The rate at which distance is covered
The rate of change of displacement with respect to time — a vector quantity with both magnitude and direction

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity = Δx/Δt. As a vector, it includes direction. Positive and negative signs indicate opposite directions along a chosen axis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes instantaneous velocity?

options.text: The velocity of an object at a single specific moment in time, equal to the derivative of position with respect to time
The peak velocity an object reaches during its entire journey, representing the single fastest point along its motion path
The total displacement divided by the total elapsed time of travel, giving the overall rate of position change for the trip
The reading obtained exclusively from a calibrated speedometer device, which only measures how fast the vehicle is currently going

quiz_questions.explanation: Instantaneous velocity v = dx/dt, the limit of Δx/Δt as Δt → 0. It is the slope of the tangent to the position-time curve at any given instant.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes uniform motion?

options.text: Motion at constant velocity — traveling in a straight line at an unchanging speed with no acceleration
Motion in a circular path at constant speed
Motion that gradually speeds up
Any motion along a straight line

quiz_questions.explanation: Uniform motion means constant velocity: zero acceleration, constant speed, and constant direction. On a position-time graph, it appears as a straight line.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a reference frame?

options.text: A coordinate system used to define positions and describe the motion of objects relative to an observer
The fixed physical boundary that separates one region of measurable space from another, often established by a visible landmark
The designated initial position from which all timed experiments begin, serving as the universal agreed-upon zero point
The downward vertical direction established by the local gravitational field, which determines how all free objects accelerate

quiz_questions.explanation: A reference frame provides the axes and origin from which positions, velocities, and accelerations are measured. Motion is always described relative to a chosen reference frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a position-time graph?

options.text: A graph showing the distance between two objects over time
A map showing where an object is located
A graph with velocity on the y-axis and time on the x-axis
A graph that plots an object's position on the vertical axis against time on the horizontal axis, where the slope represents velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: Position-time graphs encode motion information: slope = velocity, horizontal line = stationary, curved line = changing velocity. They are fundamental tools in kinematics.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the odometer vs. speedometer distinction?

options.text: An odometer measures displacement while a speedometer measures velocity
An odometer measures total distance traveled (scalar), while a speedometer measures instantaneous speed (scalar)
Both measure the same quantity in different units
An odometer measures average speed while a speedometer measures maximum speed

quiz_questions.explanation: The odometer accumulates total path length (distance — always increasing). The speedometer shows how fast the car is going right now (instantaneous speed). Neither provides directional information.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes relative velocity?

options.text: The total velocity of all objects in a system
The maximum velocity difference between two objects
The velocity of one object as observed from the reference frame of another moving object
The average of two objects' velocities

quiz_questions.explanation: Relative velocity v_A relative to B = v_A − v_B. If two cars move in the same direction at 60 km/h and 40 km/h, the relative velocity of the first from the second's frame is 20 km/h.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student walks 4 blocks north to school, then 3 blocks east to the library after school. Her pedometer (step counter) shows she walked 700 m total. What are her distance and displacement from home to the library?

options.text: Distance = 500 m (the hypotenuse of the path), Displacement = 700 m at 53.1 degrees north of east (the actual walked route)
Distance = Displacement = 500 m, since both quantities always measure the shortest straight-line route between two locations
Distance = 700 m (total path), Displacement = 500 m at 36.9° east of north (the straight-line shortcut)
Distance = Displacement = 700 m, because both quantities always give the total path length that the person walked on foot

quiz_questions.explanation: Distance is the 700 m total path (4 blocks + 3 blocks). Displacement is the straight-line distance from home to library. If blocks are 100 m: displacement = √(400² + 300²) = 500 m at tan⁻¹(300/400) = 36.9° east of north.

quiz_questions.question_text: A delivery driver drives 30 km in 30 minutes, stops for 15 minutes for a delivery, then drives 30 km back in 30 minutes. Calculate the average speed and average velocity for the entire trip.

options.text: Average speed = 48 km/h (total distance 60 km / total time 1.25 h); average velocity = 0 (displacement is zero since he returned to the start)
Average speed = 0 because the driver returned to the start; average velocity = 48 km/h based on total distance of 60 km divided by total time of 1.25 h
Average speed = average velocity = 48 km/h, since the total distance traveled was 60 km and the total elapsed time was 1.25 hours for the whole trip
Average speed = average velocity = 0, because returning to the starting point means both the distance traveled and the displacement are zero for the trip

quiz_questions.explanation: Total distance = 30 + 30 = 60 km. Total time = 30 + 15 + 30 = 75 min = 1.25 h. Average speed = 60/1.25 = 48 km/h. Displacement = 0 (returned to start), so average velocity = 0/1.25 = 0.

quiz_questions.question_text: A GPS app shows a driver has traveled 50 km using 'trip distance' but the 'displacement' reading shows only 30 km. What does this difference reveal about the driver's path?

options.text: Displacement is always exactly 60% of distance
The driver went backwards at some point, which subtracted from distance
The GPS is malfunctioning
The driver did not travel in a straight line — the path included turns, curves, or detours, making distance > displacement

quiz_questions.explanation: Distance ≥ |displacement| always. They are equal only for straight-line motion without backtracking. The 20 km difference (50 − 30) indicates the path deviated significantly from a straight line between start and end points.

quiz_questions.question_text: A sprinter runs 100 m in 10.0 s. Her coach says her average speed was 10 m/s but her instantaneous speed at the 50 m mark was 11.5 m/s. How is this possible?

options.text: She must have been running downhill at the 50 m mark which artificially raised her instantaneous speed beyond the average — on flat ground they would be equal values
The coach made a measurement error because instantaneous speed at any point during a uniform-distance run must exactly equal the average speed over the whole race
Instantaneous speed must always be identical to average speed for any straight-line motion — the higher reading at the 50 m mark was caused by a faulty timing device
Her speed varied during the race — she accelerated from rest at the start and was faster than average in the middle; instantaneous speed at a point can differ from average speed over the whole race

quiz_questions.explanation: Average speed = total distance / total time = 100/10 = 10 m/s. But she started from rest and accelerated, so she was slower at the start and faster later. At the 50 m mark, her instantaneous speed exceeded the average. Instantaneous ≠ average unless speed is constant.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two trains leave the same station. Train A travels north at 80 km/h and Train B travels south at 60 km/h. After 2 hours, what is the distance between them and why does this involve vectors?

options.text: 280 km apart — since they travel in opposite directions, their displacements add: (80 × 2) + (60 × 2) = 160 + 120 = 280 km
4800 km apart — to find separation you multiply the two speeds and then by time: (80 times 60) times 2 = 4800 km of total separation distance
140 km apart — averaging the two speeds and multiplying by time gives the gap: ((80 + 60) / 2) times 2 = 140 km between the trains
40 km apart — subtracting velocities gives the relative approach speed: (80 minus 60) times 2 = 40 km of total separation between the trains

quiz_questions.explanation: Train A displacement: 160 km north. Train B displacement: 120 km south. Since they move in opposite directions, the separation = 160 + 120 = 280 km. Vector addition: 160 north − (−120 south) = 160 + 120 = 280 km.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car's speedometer reads 90 km/h due north. At that instant, the driver turns onto an eastbound highway and maintains 90 km/h. Has the velocity changed?

options.text: No — speedometers measure velocity directly
Yes — the speed increased because east is a 'faster' direction
Yes — although the speed remained constant at 90 km/h, the direction changed from north to east, so the velocity (a vector) has changed
No — the speed stayed the same so the velocity is unchanged

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector (magnitude + direction). Speed (magnitude) stayed at 90 km/h, but direction changed from north to east. Therefore the velocity changed, which means the car experienced acceleration during the turn.

quiz_questions.question_text: An ant walks along a meter stick from the 20 cm mark to the 80 cm mark, then back to the 50 cm mark. What are the distance and displacement?

options.text: Distance = Displacement = 60 cm
Distance = 30 cm, Displacement = 90 cm
Distance = 90 cm (60 cm forward + 30 cm back); Displacement = +30 cm (from 20 cm to 50 cm mark)
Distance = 60 cm, Displacement = 0 cm

quiz_questions.explanation: Distance: |80 − 20| + |50 − 80| = 60 + 30 = 90 cm. Displacement: final − initial = 50 − 20 = +30 cm (toward the higher numbers on the stick).

quiz_questions.question_text: A race car on a 5 km circular track completes exactly 3 laps in 6 minutes. What are its average speed and average velocity?

options.text: Average speed = average velocity = 150 km/h, since speed and velocity are always identical for any object moving along a circular track path
Average speed = 150 km/h (15 km / 0.1 h); Average velocity = 0 (displacement is zero after complete laps, back to start)
Average speed = 50 km/h using one lap distance 5 km / 0.1 h; Average velocity = 50 km/h pointed in the direction the car was last heading
Average speed = 0 and Average velocity = 0, because any circular motion returns the car to the starting point after each complete lap

quiz_questions.explanation: Distance = 3 × 5 = 15 km. Time = 6 min = 0.1 h. Average speed = 15/0.1 = 150 km/h. After 3 complete laps, the car is back at the starting line, so displacement = 0 and average velocity = 0.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student plots position vs. time and gets a straight line with slope 5 m/s for the first 4 seconds, then a horizontal line from 4 to 7 seconds. What was the object doing?

options.text: Moving at a constant speed of 5 m/s for the full 7 seconds with no changes, since the position-time graph maintains the same value throughout the entire time interval
Traveling at constant speed of 5 m/s during all 7 seconds — a horizontal segment on a position-time graph simply indicates that the speed remains constant and unchanged
Accelerating at 5 m/s squared for the first 4 seconds then decelerating from 4 to 7 seconds — the slope of a position-time graph represents acceleration not velocity
Moving at constant velocity of 5 m/s for 4 seconds, then stationary (at rest) from 4 to 7 seconds — the slope gives velocity and zero slope means zero velocity

quiz_questions.explanation: On a position-time graph: constant positive slope = constant velocity (5 m/s here). Horizontal line = zero slope = zero velocity (stationary). The object traveled 20 m in 4 s, then stayed put for 3 s.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two ships leave a port simultaneously. Ship A sails 100 km east, Ship B sails 100 km north. Both take 5 hours. They have the same speed but a sailor says they have different velocities. Explain.

options.text: Correct — both have speed 20 km/h but Ship A's velocity is 20 km/h east and Ship B's is 20 km/h north; velocity includes direction, so different directions mean different velocities
The sailor is incorrect — since both ships travel 100 km in 5 hours they have the same speed of 20 km/h and therefore must also have identical velocities by definition
Same speed always implies the same velocity for any two objects covering equal distances in equal amounts of time — the travel directions do not factor into the comparison
The velocities differ only because the ships have different masses and hull designs — a heavier ship would have lower velocity even when traveling at the same measured speed

quiz_questions.explanation: Speed (scalar) = 100 km / 5 h = 20 km/h for both. Velocity (vector) = 20 km/h east for A, 20 km/h north for B. Since velocity includes direction, these are different velocity vectors despite identical speeds.
